steps.title: Use angle-angle similarity
steps.content: The triangles already have one matching angle pair: $\angle L=\angle R=58^\circ$. To prove similarity, look for one additional angle condition that makes the angle sets match.

steps.title: Apply angle-angle similarity criterion
steps.content: We already have angle L = angle R = 58 degrees. For AA similarity, we need one more pair of equal angles. The side-length options alone do not establish similarity here, so focus on the additional angle information.

steps.title: Check the candidate: angle M = 72° and angle S = 58°
steps.content:
In triangle LMN: angle L = 58 degrees, angle M = 72 degrees, so angle N = 50 degrees.
In triangle RST: angle R = 58 degrees, angle S = 58 degrees, so angle T = 64 degrees.
Triangle LMN has angles {58 degrees, 72 degrees, 50 degrees}.

Triangle RST has angles {58 degrees, 58 degrees, 64 degrees}. The angle sets differ, so the triangles are not similar.

steps.title: Check the candidate: angle M = 72° and angle T = 50°
steps.content:
In triangle LMN: angle L = 58 degrees, angle M = 72 degrees, so angle N = 50 degrees.
In triangle RST: angle R = 58 degrees, angle T = 50 degrees, so angle S = 72 degrees.
Triangle LMN has angles 58 degrees, 72 degrees, 50 degrees at vertices L, M, N.

Triangle RST has angles 58 degrees, 72 degrees, 50 degrees at vertices R, S, T.

All angles match under the correspondence L ↔ R, M ↔ S, N ↔ T.

By AA similarity, the triangles are similar. That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
